events.title: War in Iran disrupts global energy markets via Strait of Hormuz risk
events.summary: An escalation of military conflict in Iran is threatening the Strait of Hormuz, which handles roughly 20% of global oil supply. Oil prices have risen sharply, driving a repricing of inflation expectations, consumer sentiment deterioration, and a reassessment of Fed policy trajectory. This is the dominant macro event shaping all asset classes.
events.why_now: The conflict escalated in March 2026, with nearly two-thirds of the University of Michigan's March survey responses collected after the escalation. Oil prices remain elevated as of March 30, and the pass-through to gasoline prices and broader inflation is actively unfolding.
events.market_relevance: Energy price spikes compress consumer discretionary spending, raise input costs across sectors, push short-term inflation expectations to 3.8% (from 3.4%), and delay expected Fed rate cuts. The S&P 500 is down ~8% from January highs and Nasdaq down >10%. Traditional diversification has broken down with stocks, bonds, and gold all under pressure simultaneously.

events.title: FOMC holds rates steady, dot plot converges on at most one cut in 2026
events.summary: The March 2026 FOMC meeting left rates unchanged. The dot plot now shows concentrated views around one cut for the remainder of 2026, with markets aligning to this view. Core PCE at 3.1% and the Iran-driven energy shock have pushed rate cut expectations further out. The probability of a hike remains low but the window for easing has narrowed significantly.
events.why_now: The March 18 FOMC decision and press conference crystallized the shift from markets pricing two cuts to one-or-none. Tariff effects have not yet fully fed through to inflation, and the Iran conflict adds a new inflationary impulse on top of already-sticky core PCE.
events.market_relevance: Higher-for-longer rates directly impact equity valuations (especially growth/tech), corporate borrowing costs, and mortgage rates. Bond fund inflows slowed to $2.5B while short-duration funds attracted a record $11.1B, signaling a defensive posture. Treasury yields have moved higher.

events.title: February nonfarm payrolls show unexpected loss of 92,000 jobs
events.summary: The February jobs report showed the US economy losing 92,000 jobs versus an expected gain of 55,000, with unemployment ticking up to 4.4%. This was a significant negative surprise that initially drove rate cut expectations higher before the Iran conflict shifted the calculus toward inflation concerns.
events.why_now: The data was released in early March and shaped pre-FOMC market positioning. The labor market weakness is now competing with inflationary pressures from the Iran conflict, creating a stagflationary dynamic that complicates Fed decision-making.
events.market_relevance: A weakening labor market reduces consumer spending power, threatens corporate revenue growth, and raises recession risk. Combined with persistent inflation, it creates a stagflation scenario where the Fed has limited room to act in either direction.

events.title: Fed Chair Powell pledges to stay amid DOJ investigation; Warsh nomination stalled
events.summary: Fed Chair Jay Powell is pledging to remain in his position until a DOJ investigation concludes or is dismissed. Senator Thom Tillis is blocking the Senate advancement of Kevin Warsh's nomination as Powell's successor. Warsh has historically been hawkish but has recently adopted a more dovish stance aligned with the President's preference for rate cuts.
events.why_now: The political standoff is active as of the March 18 FOMC press conference. The uncertainty around Fed leadership creates policy ambiguity at a critical juncture when markets need clarity on the inflation-vs-growth tradeoff.
events.market_relevance: Fed leadership uncertainty adds a risk premium to rate-sensitive assets. If Warsh eventually takes over with a dovish mandate, it could signal politically-influenced monetary easing, potentially weakening the dollar and boosting inflation expectations. Markets must price in regime change risk.

events.title: Tariff effects have not yet fully passed through to consumer prices
events.summary: Multiple sources note that the full inflationary impact of existing tariffs has not yet been realized. Core PCE rose to 3.1% in January even before the Iran conflict, suggesting a structural inflation floor that tariffs will continue to elevate. This creates a compounding inflation problem layered on top of the energy shock.
events.why_now: The March FOMC meeting explicitly acknowledged this lag. With inflation already persistent at 3.1% core PCE, additional tariff pass-through represents an upside inflation risk that could further delay rate cuts or even reintroduce hike discussions.
events.market_relevance: Companies facing tariff-driven input cost increases will see margin compression unless they can pass costs to consumers, which risks demand destruction in a weakening labor market. Import-heavy sectors (retail, autos, tech hardware) are most exposed.

events.title: University of Michigan consumer sentiment drops to 53.3, expectations component falls 9%
events.summary: The final March University of Michigan consumer sentiment reading fell to 53.3 from 56.6 in February, a 6% monthly decline. The expectations component dropped nearly 9%, reflecting growing pessimism about the near-term economic outlook driven by energy prices and market volatility. One-year inflation expectations jumped to 3.8%.
events.why_now: The final March reading captures post-Iran-escalation sentiment. The sharp deterioration in expectations signals that consumers are beginning to pull back, which could translate into reduced spending in coming months.
events.market_relevance: Consumer spending drives roughly 70% of US GDP. A sharp decline in sentiment, particularly the forward-looking expectations component, is a leading indicator for retail sales weakness, potential earnings misses in consumer-facing sectors, and broader economic deceleration.

events.title: Record $37.2B weekly inflow to US equities alongside record $11.1B into short-duration bonds
events.summary: For the week ending March 25, US equity funds saw $37.2B in inflows (largest in 2.5 months), reversing a multi-week outflow trend. Simultaneously, short-duration bond funds attracted a record $11.1B while high-yield credit saw $4.8B in outflows. European equity funds lost $7.5B. This signals tactical repositioning rather than capitulation.
events.why_now: The flows occurred in the final week of March as markets digested the Iran conflict, FOMC decision, and equity selloff. The simultaneous equity buying and defensive bond positioning suggests institutional investors are selectively adding risk while hedging duration and credit exposure.
events.market_relevance: The divergence between equity inflows and high-yield outflows suggests a quality rotation within risk assets. The record short-duration inflows confirm that institutional capital is prioritizing liquidity. European outflows may reflect relative geopolitical risk repricing given proximity to the Middle East conflict.

events.title: Stocks, bonds, and gold all decline simultaneously as real yields and dollar strengthen
events.summary: A rare environment has emerged where equities, Treasuries, and gold are all under pressure at the same time. Rising real yields and a 2-3% stronger US dollar have increased the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets, while inflation repricing has pushed bond prices lower. Traditional portfolio hedges are failing.
events.why_now: This dynamic intensified through March as the Iran conflict drove inflation expectations higher while the Fed signaled no imminent easing. The simultaneous failure of traditional hedges is forcing portfolio rebalancing and creating stress in multi-asset strategies.
events.market_relevance: When diversification breaks down, volatility-targeting strategies and risk-parity funds are forced to deleverage, amplifying selling pressure. The stronger dollar pressures multinational earnings and emerging market debt. Investors are being pushed into cash and short-duration instruments as the only effective hedge.
